content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What older movies are better understood as an adult?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Hello! Wondering which movies one may have watched as a kid(of the 80s), but hit different as an adult? 

Like a romance that was too mushy for kids, or a comedy that was too adult for a kid to understand. 

Some films you just write off. I want to know which ones may deserve a second watch (or a first)! I have watched most all mainstream movies.

Not action, because those I could appreciate at any age! No slapstick comedies either. Looking for more cerebral ones or adult humor.

I apologize if this is too vague.

Ex. Cocoon, gods must be crazy= like 

Fried green tomatoes, driving miss daisy= need a rewatch.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Princess Bride, 1987
2. So for really old, It’s a Wonderful Life, for old but more modern, Office Space. 
  - It’s a Wonderful Life is a great shoutout 
3. Ferris bueller day off

I used to think Cameron was lame. Now I feel like Ferris is kind of a jerk. 
  - My wife hates Ferris with a passion. But, she's a Tiger Mom who hates the idea of lazy students neglecting their education. 
4. So, hear me out … Rosemary’s Baby. I’m Gen X and first saw it in my early twenties. Rosemary was a weak character to me, I was screaming GET AWAY FROM HIM WHAT like the whole time. In the interim, I’ve watched several more times, and I now see her strength and resolve. And understand why she’d still cuddle her child at the end. Why she stayed, why she did all that. Trapped (in the early sixties) as a housewife, with “well meaning” neighbors, being isolated from her college friends, recommended a satanic dr - it was gradual, how would she know? 


5. Grosse Pointe Blank. Just better as an adult.
6. Hook. It was wrongly marketed as a kids movie. The real message is for adults.
7. Footloose was the first movie I saw in the theater without my parents; I was 7 and I saw it with two friends from my class. I thought it was a lot of fun, there was a good guy (Bacon) and a bad guy (Lithgow), and the good guy won and everyone had fun and Ren was right all along… right? When I rewatched it as an adult, I won’t say that Lithgow’s Reverend Moore was “right”, but I was really blown away at how well Lithgow portrayed him. He made me understand why he was so protective in an emotional way, and I could really empathize and see where he was coming from. As a kid all I wanted to see was older kids dancing and having fun, but as an adult who understands responsibility and fear of reckless behavior, I started to see Lithgow as the high point of the movie and it was his scenes I found myself waiting for. I’m not gonna say that Footloose is this secretly deep masterpiece of a movie, but it sure made me appreciate John Lithgow as an actor so much more than I already did! 
8. Giving away some spoilers for those who haven’t seen these movies.

Bull Durham (1988) as a teenager, I was disappointed Crash Davis didn’t “win” as a player. I expected him to get to the major leagues because he earned it by working hard and being talented but as an adult I understand that isn’t always enough. I like to think he made it as a manager. 

Color of Money (1986) same as the above. As a kid, I was disappointed “Fast” Eddie didn’t win the pool tournament but as an adult I understand him being back in the game was the win. 

City Slickers (1991) I found the movie very funny as a teenager and I understood the humor of getting old and being unsatisfied with where you are in life. As an adult I better understand the “ Have you ever had that feeling that this is the best I'm ever gonna do, this is the best I'm ever gonna feel... and it ain't that great” but I also understand find that one thing- or person- that can make you happy. 


## 2. Looking for Movies like Dead Poets Society, Little Women, Your Name, The Tree of Life?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Hi everyone,  
I’m looking for film recommendations based on a mix of emotional, reflective, and visually meaningful movies. I also like indie-feeling films.

Some films I love include:   
*- Dead Poets Society*  
*- Mona Lisa Smile*  
*- Little Women*  
*- Your Name*

And I also enjoy more documentary-like, contemplative, and real life-focused films such as:

*- Crows Are White*  
*- Babies*  
*- The Tree of Life*

I tend to be drawn to films that feel introspective, emotionally grounded, visually beautiful, or philosophical.

I’m open to any suggestions(drama, animation, documentary, indie, etc.)

Thanks in advance for any recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What Dreams May Come.

The Fisher King

Tender Mercies
2. Good Will Hunting 👍
3. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013)

In Bruges (2008) - This is also a crime film with the violence that comes with that genre. But it's done in a very poignant and thought provoking way.

The Wind Rises (2013)
4. Now & Then
5. The old 'The Bad News Bears' is "introspective, emotionally grounded, visually beautiful, or philosophical" but it is subtle.
  - I used to think Frank Capra best captured America with Meet John Doe and It’s A Wonderful Life.  But then The Bad News Bears (1976) came along a perfectly captured American upper middle class suburban striving juxtaposed with lower middle class survival. 

If a foreigner wants to understand America, The Bad News Bears is where it’s at. 
6. The Holdovers

Whiplash

Dan in Real Life

The Way Way Back
7. After Life (1998) the Japanese film. one of the rare films that makes you appreciate the everyday moments. Brings a tear to my eye at every watching. Many of the interviews are non actors, as the filmmakers interviewed hundreds of people for their memories. 

In The Mood for Love  (2000) a masterpiece about connection, love, and regret. the ending always shatters me. 2046 is the sequel. Both are visually magnificent, wonderful soundtracks, and so emotional. 
8. Troop Beverly Hills.
9. Big Fish. And With Honors. 

## 3. Movie suggestions to help teens with topics they don’t like talking to adults about?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Hello Reddit.

I’m a pastoral lead for year 9 (13-14 year olds). I’m starting a movie club with some of my students and I am looking for films that could help them deal with things they may be experiencing but don’t quite know how to process.

Films helped me a lot in my teen years to process things, and I think my students will get a lot out of this too.

I am looking for movies that deal with subjects obviously in an age appropriate way and all the films will be approved by myself, the head and parents will have final say.

Themes I am interested in covering are -  
Bullying  
Peer pressure  
Gender & sexuality  
Relationships  
Family dynamics and change in the family dynamic  
Inappropriate relationships  
Teen pregnancy  
Homelessness  
And anything else that you also think may have helped you deal with something as a teen.

If you’re so inclined a brief anecdote of how a movie helped you through a situation would be amazing too, I’d like to talk to the students before and after about the themes of the movie and what feelings it may invoke.

Your help would be greatly appreciated Reddit.

EDIT: I am not a pastor. I am a pastoral lead. I am a head of year in a British school, it’s nothing to do with religion, it’s the name we call none teaching positions however we still have interactions with the children. I am basically the manager/mentor/mother while at school of a bunch of 13-14 year olds. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Saved!
2. Dead Poets Society
3. The Hate U Give
4. Easy A, 
Juno, 
Stand by Me, 
Mean Girls
  - Juno also has a great warning about Jason Batemans character. 
5. The Edge of Seventeen (2016) - Grief, impulse decision-making, struggles with very close friends, sibling friction, family struggles after losing a loved one.

The Breakfast Club (1985) - Detention, unhelpful authority figures, unseen life struggles, parental pressure. 

Fyi these are both R rated.
6. Enemy Mine (1985)

Covers all the categories except teen pregnancy (but does cover pregnancy).     watched it during religion class back in the 90s.
7. Big Fish.  Second Hand Lions.  Jesus Revolution. Ben Hur the old version.  Sandlot.
8. These are not rated R:

Wonder 2017: bullying, empathy 

Pursuit of Happyness 2006: Homelessness, single parent
9. “White Squall” from the 90’s, starring Jeff Bridges. A true story of adventure, character building and friendship. I believe it’s on Tubi 👍🥹

## 4. Movies like The Silence of the Lambs or Prisoners

**Score:** 16
**Body:** In the mood for movies that are set around small towns, winter time, with a psychological tension and crime as the theme.  Along the lines of The Snowman or Fargo. Nordic Noir.  Lesser known, the better. TIA.

EDIT: All these suggestions are great!  Thanks so much everyone.  Keep ‘em coming.  The cold  is setting in Aust so I’ll need a long list to watch and stay indoors!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Red Rooms (2023)
  - This movie is a masterpiece and I would kill to see it again for the first time. 
2. Winter's Bone
3. A Simple Plan
4. Watch the tv series 'Trapped'. from Iceland. It's one of the best I've seen.
5. Manhunter
6. The Long Kiss Goodnight 
7. Girl with a Dragon Tattoo isn’t exactly lesser known, but either version checks the other boxes
8. Insomnia (2002)
9. Insomnia 

## 5. Movies not for kids but I can watch around kids

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Thanks so much everyone! I now have a great list of movies to watch

Im looking for movie recs that are ok to watch around my 8 year old, that aren't for kids if that makes sense. I feel like I have watched every kid and family movie there is and my kid doesn't even watch what im watching anyway. But I struggle to find movies for adults that dont have sex or gore in it. Im not too worried about swearing because its probably nothing they haven't heard from me already. Any recommendations are appreciated of any genre!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Movies where people sit in a room and talk about weighty subjects. No gore, no sex, maybe some bad language depending on the film. Captivating to adults, but an 8 year old walking into the room would be bored and wander off. 

Conclave (Actually rated PG, surprisingly)

12 Angry Men

Conspiracy

One Night in Miami

Margin Call

Moneyball

The Social Network


2. Movie musicals like Guys and Dolls. Cave of Forgotten Dreams. The Princess Bride. 


3. Most old black and white movies would fit the bill, if you’re into that kind of thing. Me, I love old movies!
4. My boy is 8 now too, I'm gearing up to watch the Lord Of The Rings trilogy with him soon. There's some dark stuff in there that could be scary for kids, depending on the individual & what they've already seen, but I think he's ready!

(For context, he's been watching the Backrooms youtube videos a lot on his mum's phone - which I wasn't happy to find out, but he's clearly taken an interest in more edgy & scary things. To be fair I was watching FAR worse at his age lol!)
5. Yellow Submarine (1968) and A Hard Day's Night  (1964) bonus: Beatles songs

About A Boy (2002) the fruitcake omg

Time Bandits (1981) Sean Connery is perfectly cast as Agamemnon. It's got a sting in its tail, making it a life lessons film.

American Splendor (2003) based on Harvey Pekar's life. the animated sections are great
6. Back to the Future

Princess Bride
7. O Brother Where Art Thou

Field of Dreams

Tree of Life




8. A League of Their Own and most Tom Hanks films. 
9. Try The Truman Show  or Enola Holmes (1 & 2) 
10. Secondhand Lions

## 4. Actors/actresses that have had just confusing careers. Ben Foster for example…

**Score:** 565
**Body:** Ben Foster. Like I just don’t get it. Is it a bad agent thing? Because it seems like every time I see him in something I think he’s really good and steals the show, then I always seem to check his IMDb and it’s movies like Hell Or High Water and then like 4-5 straight to DVD bargain bin movies. It’s just confusing. I guess everyone has bills? 🤷🏽‍♂️

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pandorum is severely  underrated.
  - Yessss I came here to post that 

Really great ending to the film as well. 
2. Him in 3:10 to Yuma is absolutely outstanding 
  - He is absolutely chilling in that and that makes his sudden vulnerability, confusion, and hurt that much better at the end when he realizes what is going on.
    - He plays a decent psycho in Hostage too
3. Alpha Dog 
4. In the immortal words of Jeremy Irons, "Are you kidding? I'd just bought a castle, I had to pay for it somehow!"
5. You ever think why so many actors have trash movies after they win an Oscar? It’s because they had to negotiate the role they want with studios that want their dancing monkeys. Yes it’s money, but they contractually obligate themselves to unknown movies for future date to get the chance to do the indy project. 

Could be much worse. The studios used to figuratively own actors like slave labor like Judy Garland. 
6. Also 30 Days of Night, like dude
7. Really made Hell or High Water a stand out movie for me.

That’s just Evan Peters, in everything

There’s one incredibly strange season of American Horror Story (they’re all fucked I know) where he plays this hideous far-right incel demagogue who recruits people and it’s chillingly effective. He’d have made a fine Joker. 

2. ![gif](giphy|PRbmtnMCcPXbmI9ssi)


3. From the same movie...

https://i.redd.it/j36m6ag40l1h1.gif
4. The opening to Valerian City of a Thousand planets   
[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yaGy7rwjsFE](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yaGy7rwjsFE)

6. Matrix 3 - the Chain Gun Mechs. It was the weakest of the trilogy, but that scene was excellent 
7. Every Zack Snyder movie! Amazing visuals crap story!!
  - Ngl, I liked Snyder in the 2000’s.

Dawn of the dead, 300, watchmen, etc.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Kristen Stewart's "I just don’t think that it’s possible to create sort of radical, vital work under capitalistic parameters."

**Score:** 442
**Body:** I don't know if anyone talks in a more refreshing way about film than Kristen Stewart, the above quote from [her recent Variety interview](https://variety.com/2026/film/festivals/kristen-stewart-cannes-full-phil-hollywood-studios-1236750708/). What do you all think about the above sentiment? I mean clearly capitalism has been wholeheartedly behind so much of what film even is, from the Old Hollywood system to all manners of its consumption in various decades. In some sense the entire auteur and indie spirit could be said to be parasitic on capitalist-driven filmmaking and consumption. I do though think that she's choosing her words carefully - and almost always does. She is saying capitalist **parameters**. That is "profit first and foremost" goal-setting or at least project shaping. If she has a point is this something that has always been the case? Have not many classic film genres, let's say 1930s Horror, or 1940-1950s Film Noir been pretty much (?) under capitalist parameters of profit chasing? Are today's film making parameters different and more corrosive? Is she just "fighting the studios" just like Cassavetes, Peckinpah and so many others did so passionately? Or is there actually a closing down of the artistic capacity of film in our era? Returning to her thoughts, she seems to be referring to a kind of Bro Capitalism. I've seen someone amazing like Kelly Reichardt say that most of the time she can't even find funding to make her fairly low-cost movies.

As we move towards a globalized (culture-hopping), digital platform driven, social media defined, streaming, big data semiocapitalism, is some important aspect of film being threatened?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't know that she's "fighting the studios" but she points out that something has changed in our era.

\> Like we can’t shoot in L.A., it’s absolutely impossible and it’s where our fucking entire business was born. There’s no way to play the game anymore.

She's got a point. Hollywood became so successful that it can no longer function anymore. Blockbuster films that in the golden age would **have** to be created in Hollywood now come from New Zealand, Hungary, Seoul, etc. For the industry and the art form, this is a good thing. But for the Hollywood system, it's not. 
  - Yes and,

>**The studios aren’t really even in Cannes this year!**

>It’s OK, because guess what happens when things break or die? It’s good, I think. Especially because I’ve been working since I was 9, I see a horizon that doesn’t resemble anything that I’ve seen before. It’s so new because our industry is so utterly devastated. And so I think actually, there’s so much hope in that. We just have to grab it.

She's talking about big structural changes happening.
2. Artists have been making these same complaints since Grok demanded Urga paint what Grok want, not what Urga want.

That being said, it always needs to be said. We always need artists saying the status quo isn’t good enough. You only get what you demand. Stewart is playing an old but necessary role in the arts. Good for her.
3. To me, honestly, the phrase sounds almost like a truism. “Capitalistic parameters” meaning market economy is risk averse when it comes to art.  
  
Specificaly on cinema, it’s a collective work that, on a large industrial scale (large studios and streaming companies), employ a lot of human capital (AI apologists are trying to change that, though), which requires some kind of safety net. Unless you have, say, state subside (and cinema often relies on it, partly or even greatly), you’re relying on market economy to make these works pay themselves.  
  
So, yes, naturally, lots of economic constraints. This doesn’t mean that one cannot achieve some level of artistic liberty at some point. But this is extremely hard and far from what’s to be expected by an artist these days.
4. A lot of people here are misinterpreting her quote. This isn’t so much about whether art is possible within a capitalist construct, but rather about how radical a film’s stance and ideology can be and whether it’s possible for such a film to be produced and distributed under capitalist circumstances.
  - Agree. She is making a finer point. She says "radical", but she also talks about wanting to make "weird" films. I'm not sure she is talking about politically radical, at least not in the sense of an overtly ideological film (though everything is "ideological" now). She explicitly talks about not wanting to wait 5 years for a million dollars for a film. Not so much that her more overt political ideas are being silenced somehow, but maybe so. LGBTQ subject matter, for instance, is now very political.
    - Weird can be a preliminary stage of the radical a concept adjacent to it, but still operating within economically feasible circumstances like quentin dupieux.

Thinking about the term “radical,” the concept of it relates to our assumed reality, where something radical would be so extremely distant and different from it that it would directly threaten our conception of reality itself.

Personally, when it comes to radical film, I think this would most likely refer to cinema that is either ideologically radical in its treatment of the subject matter or formally radical in its cinematic language and structure.

The question is more if she means by "capitalistic structures" the general economic incentives of radical films or the existence of radical films completely detached from film and film adjacent industries
5. George Lucas in 2015:

"In the world we live in - and the system we've created for ourselves, in terms of it's a big industry - you cannot lose money. So the point is that you're forced to make a particular kind of movie. And I used to say this all the time, with people, you know, back when Russia was the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, and they'd say: "Oh, but aren’t you so glad that you're in America?" And I'd say: "Well, I know a lot of Russian filmmakers and they have a lot more freedom than I have. All they have to do is be careful about criticizing the government. Otherwise, they can do anything they want"

Source: https://charlierose.com/videos/23471
6. I belive those works can be made. However, they might not have the budget and distribution that you want. Bleach from nirvana cost $600 and was arguably a better artistic statement than nevermind, which with a $100,000 budget, was still relatively cheap at the time. You can still make works of art. You cant guarantee "success".
Edit: i will say i was replying to the cherry picked headline and not her actual interview/argument.
7. The "change" was the telecommunications act of 96 that lightened the restrictions on studio consolidation. So a variety of things happened after mergers and buyouts that kind of restrict what's funded and how they're made. So you have to consider the amount of debt companies incur when they acquire other companies, what types of investments they take on and what type of risks they're willing to take with the ips they acquire. I think a lot of studios that have accrued massive amounts of debt would prefer to use the minimal viable product model and just let fan requests spearhead the creative process, and that's possibly why there's so much lack of intent with those acquired ips in comparison to how they were managed when their original creators were in control, in the latter case you see more innovation and traditional storytelling but in the former you see a lot of nostalgia and risk aversion. How these tentpoles push out original works at the theater is another issue. Relying on fan feedback to help compete with the attention economy across social media is a losing battle imo.

Smaller studios don't have this problem because they have to create from scratch and hope for the promise of a franchiseable idea.

So I agree with Kristen but only in the sense that this is a different form of capitalism than what we were previously used too. Hollywood and Disney prior to the French New Wave already had studio led films with minimal authorship then after the wave there was more artistic expression of the individual or group of individuals. Now with these studios they're more like acquisition companies employing the artistic expression of popular opinion. A fan may be able to be creative but fans in general cannot write and have some pretty predictible requests, and that's seems to be the chosen method to take on social media to try to force people away from their phones and into theaters and I don't think it works but you have to remember the debt and trying to ensure ROI.

## 2. Casual Discussion Thread (May 16, 2026)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)
* [Twitter](https://twitter.com/truefilmreddit)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Should I watch the human condition trilogy separately or in one sitting for my first time?

**Score:** 4
**Body:** Title. I've got no problem with watching hours and hours of films back to back, as a lord of the rings fan you wouldn't expect me to. I'm finally about to get round to the human condition trilogy, but I hear it's another one that was directed and filmed as one film. Letterboxd has them all as individual films as they were released, but also they have war and peace all as one 7 hour film. Which route do I go for? What will give me the best experience. I understand it'll be quite a heavy film so I might be a bit dead and depressed by the end if I do it in one, was this the case for you if you watched it this way? Thanks

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. i watched them separately, but on near-consecutive days without watching anything else in between, and can recommend that approach. each part stands as its own story, and i appreciated having some time to digest and process each part before moving onto the next.
2. I watched it in one go (maybe like 30 min break in between) and my feeling is that whether you break it up or not has no bearing on how much it'll crush you by the end, so I recommend you do the same as I did :)
3. I watched them separately, and even paused for my own intermission during the 2nd and 3rd films.  The films tend to be be unrelenting and bleak.  I believe I would start to get numb to what is presented if I did not step away, and the films are worth my full attention.
4. I'd say, doesn't really matter. They tell one long story but they're pretty self contained parts, they flow well into each other but you don't *need* to do it all in one go. When I watched them for the first time, I believe I was planning on spread them across a long weekend and then ended up bingeing them all in one day. They're heavy, but not "I'm gonna turn off the TV and sit here for a while" heavy (mostly). Personally, I wouldn't do something like "part 1 today, part 2 next month, part 3 three months after that", I'd try to at least watch them in quick succession, but originally they were released from '59 to '61, and people who watched them like that still liked them. They're gonna be great films, no matter how you watch 'em.
5. Watch them in three consecutive evenings. I feel this is the best compromise between pacing and continuity. It gives your brain time to digest what you just watched, but not so long that you will start to forget details.
6. It's very episodic, almost like a proto-miniseries. Each part has title cards for different segments and has a definitive ending leading to the next one. Watching it in one sitting wouldn't be advisable, in my opinion. It's not just the length. The films are really dense, exploring a variety of complex situations and moral dilemmas. Going through all 9 hours in one-sitting, while impressive, would risk missing a lot of the ideas Kobayashi explored.
7. My son is a film buff and he introduced it to me last year. I wasn't interested at the time (he was midway through the third film at the time), but he managed to convince me to start at the beginning. 

We watched half of each movie (roughly 90 minutes) each night over a week, skipping a day here and there. By the end of the first half of the first film, I was hooked. I usually am not a fan of massive sagas, but THC is really well done.

## 4. Favorite Michael Dudikoff Film?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** [Bounty Hunters 2: Hardball (1997) - Movie Trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_0kK62ghZnk)

Mine is Bounty Hunters 2: Hardball.

This is one of my favorite bad movies ever that I used to watch over and over again. It's a shame it's not available on any streaming platform, the only way to see it today is via a cheap DVD copy if you can find it on the internet. Nobody posted the full film on YouTube or anything, but everybody on this sub should see it. The first film is bad and funny too, but the second one runs circles around it, just way more going on and doesn't take itself as seriously. The second one is basically a parody (in a subtle way) of the serious action film the first one tried and failed to be, except it has all the qualities us bad movie lovers appreciate in awful obscure action movies. It has a ton of replay value and I feel like I'm one of the only people who's seen it. This made me a Michael Dudikoff fan, this guy is great in self aware action movies where he's kind of a stumbling doofus who can still kick your ass and somehow survive the most ridiculous situations like 007.

TL;DR - Bounty Hunters 2: Hardball is unironically one of the best and most underrated bad movies in existence and it's criminally unavailable and underseen, but well worth checking out for adventurous viewers and fans of Michael Dudikoff!

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought Naomi's Smile 2 performance was incredible. However, Inde's was on a different level; there's no world, IMO, where there's even a sliver of debate on what performance stole the show.

I was in awe at how natural Inde effortlessly switched from normal-ish, odd, alarming, uh wtf?, unnerving, to dread-inducing. Few performances have stuck with me for so long.

Naomi had a blueprint, many are familiar with, for her character -- pop star with slight diva tendencies, on her redemption/comeback tour, she's loud, she's fragile, and she's having a really bad day -- and she followed the blueprint perfectly with some excellent intensity. However, swap her with any talented actress, and you'd likely get the same performance -- like a lot of pop songs, her performance was enjoyable and fun but she was ultimately forgettable not long after the credits rolled. 

I can't imagine Obsession's Nicki being played by anyone but Inde, she made that character her own. Judging by all the posts, buzz, and reviews, there seems to be a lot of viewers who are still thinking about Inde's performance long after watching the movie. 

  
Just my two cents. I think we both agree that both Naomi and Inde are extremely talented actresses. 
  - Personally, I think both actresses gave it a lot in terms of raw emotion and insanity. I would give Naomi the edge due to the musical and choreographed scenes in Smile 2. She did both. You can find an actress that can do one or the other but I think it’s very hard to find a comparable actress that can do both to that degree. But we can agree for sure that these are two amazing actresses. Thanks for sharing your thoughts!
